coredata.dc:title: Sulfur-acetylacetone based solutions for precipitation of quasi-core–shell microparticles or hybrid structures
coredata.dc:creator: Zaman, Ali Can
coredata.dc:description: A methodology to produce quasi-core–shell or hybrid microstructured sulfur particles is introduced in this study. Dissolution of sulfur in acetylacetone (AcAc) suspensions at high temperatures and recrystallization upon cooling enables production of quasi-spherical carbon–sulfur composites or carbon nanotube (CNT)/graphite encapsulated sulfur particles. Particle sizes are in the range of several micrometers in all cases. The particle formation mechanism relies on temperature dependent solubility of sulfur in AcAc. AcAc provides dissolution of sulfur at relatively high quantities (greater than25 g L-1), and it also allows homogenous dispersion of nanoparticles. This medium provides interaction of sulfur crystallites with carbon particles during recrystallization in colloid. Several techniques were employed to elucidate the solubility behavior of sulfur in AcAc and structure of composites including fluorescence spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering (DLS) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). It was found that sulfur presents in the form of homocyclic rings in AcAc near room temperature under ambient pressure as verified through assignments using UV–Visible absorption (UV–Vis) and Raman spectroscopy techniques. This study has wide implications. Because exploitation of high solubility of sulfur in polar and non-volatile solvents may provide new opportunities for composite particle production with tailored properties.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of major steps of S-CQD or S-CNT particle production through dissolution–recrystallization mechanism.

(a) Optical image of supersaturated sulfur in AcAc at room temperature, (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of AcAc solutions having different S content, (c) PL spectra of pure AcAc and sulfur dissolved AcAc, inset is comparison of PL spectrum of S-AcAc solutions with that of CQD-PC suspension, (d) DLS correlation decay curves of commercial polystyrene latex suspension, pure AcAc and sulfur saturated AcAc, (e) ATR-FTIR spectra of AcAc and room temperature stable S-AcAc solution, (f) Raman Spectra of AcAc and S-AcAc solutions. S saturated AcAc means unstable sulfur solutions cooling from high temperature (100 °C) to room temperature and it signifies that there may be certain amount of precipitation during analysis.

(a) Low and (b) high magnification SEM images of solvothermally synthesized carbon powder. (c) and (d) are SEM images top region of membrane filter used to filter CQD-PC suspension revealing polymer condensates that have size larger than 200 nm. (e) is EDS spectrum and elemental distribution. (f1) is high wavenumber region and (f2) is fingerprint region of ATR-FTIR spectrum of CQD-PC sample. (g) is XRD pattern of CQD-PC sample.

(a) Optical images of colloid of CQD-PC under visible or UV light irradiation (b) TEM image of diluted colloidal sample (inset is diffraction pattern). (c) is AFM image of colloidal sample and selected line traces. (d) is corresponding height profiles of line traces in the AFM image. (e) UV–Vis spectra of CQD-PC colloids, (f) PL emission behavior of CQD-PC as a function of excitation wavelength at pH 7 and (g) emission behavior of colloid at different pH values.
(a) BSE image of S-Na, (b) BSE image of S-CQD, (c-d) BSE image of S-CQD-Na (inset is particle size distribution). (e) and (f) are relatively high magnification SE images of S-CQD-Na.

(a) and (c) SE image of S-CQD-Na, (b) overlay X-ray elemental map of the same image, (d) carbon X-ray map in (c). (e) is EDS spectra of S-CQD and S-CQD-Na. The inset is elemental distribution of carbon, oxygen and sulfur in particles. (f) is ATR-FTIR spectra of elemental sulfur, CQD-PC and S-CQD-Na.
(a) and (d) are SEM image of core–shell S-CNT particles, and (e) and (f) are S-CNT-PVP particles.

head.ce:title: Sulfur-acetylacetone based solutions for precipitation of quasi-core–shell microparticles or hybrid structures

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Science and Technology Application and Research Center, Yildiz Technical University, Esenler-34220, Istanbul, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Science and Technology Application and Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Science and Technology Application and Research Center, Yildiz Technical University, Esenler, Istanbul 34200, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A methodology to produce quasi-core–shell or hybrid microstructured sulfur particles is introduced in this study. Dissolution of sulfur in acetylacetone (AcAc) suspensions at high temperatures and recrystallization upon cooling enables production of quasi-spherical carbon–sulfur composites or carbon nanotube (CNT)/graphite encapsulated sulfur particles. Particle sizes are in the range of several micrometers in all cases. The particle formation mechanism relies on temperature dependent solubility of sulfur in AcAc. AcAc provides dissolution of sulfur at relatively high quantities (greater than25 g L-1), and it also allows homogenous dispersion of nanoparticles. This medium provides interaction of sulfur crystallites with carbon particles during recrystallization in colloid. Several techniques were employed to elucidate the solubility behavior of sulfur in AcAc and structure of composites including fluorescence spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering (DLS) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). It was found that sulfur presents in the form of homocyclic rings in AcAc near room temperature under ambient pressure as verified through assignments using UV–Visible absorption (UV–Vis) and Raman spectroscopy techniques. This study has wide implications. Because exploitation of high solubility of sulfur in polar and non-volatile solvents may provide new opportunities for composite particle production with tailored properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials can be synthesized in different forms which can adopt wide range of crystalline and non-crystalline structures [1] and morphologies. Carbon (CQDs) or graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are among those. The accidental discovery of fluorescing CQDs was realized by analyzing byproducts in arc-discharged soot generated for the synthesis of CNTs [2,3]. After this discovery, CQDs/GQDs have been explored and used in numerous applications like photocatalysis [4], bio-imaging [5], sensors [6–8], electrochemical [9–16], surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) platforms [17] and carbon dot films for light emitters [18,19]. CQDs/GQDs can be utilized as additive, coating or matrix material in composite applications [11–14,20]. The brilliant electronic properties of CQDs as electron donors and acceptors endow them with wide potential for their use as electrodes in energy storage devices like batteries and supercapacitors [9]. For this purpose, hybrid nanostructured materials could be successfully prepared by coupling CQDs with electroactive materials. Because appropriate type of CQD coatings or networks may enable fast ion transport during redox reactions.

CNTs are special type of carbon allotropes as in the case of CQDs. They are wrapped up sheets of graphene planes. They found use in many applications from microwave absorption [21] and electronics [22] to battery applications [23]. Formation of functional hybrids/composites is possible due to high electrical conductivity and high aspect ratio of CNTs.

Excess amount of elemental sulfur is generated annually from hydrodesulfurization in petroleum refining processes [24]. Sulfur can be used in many applications, Lithium-Sulfur battery application is one of the notable examples. Because Lithium-Sulfur battery has high theoretical energy density of 2600 W h kg−1 and low cost [25]. However, sulfur cannot be used on its own in many applications. Such as blending with conducting agents is required in battery applications. In addition, wrapping sulfur with a suitable material is needed to avoid generation of metal-sulfur colloidal particle formation during Cd2+ ion removal from water [26]. Thereby, functional sulfur composite production is crucial in many applications. Besides, the methods should be facile. Carbon materials stand out as functional additives for sulfur in many applications. Different configurations of carbon and sulfur can be realized. It is possible to produce three dimensional sandwiches [27], loading of sulfur in pores of carbons [28], encapsulation of sulfur, core–shell particle production [29] and interlayer formation [30].

Solution processing can be an effective strategy to form sulfur particles [31–34]. It is not only beneficial for chemical synthesis [35] but also can be beneficial for composite particle production. Solution processing is possible and applicable for material production when sufficient sulfur solubility is attained in suitable solvents. There can be various strategies for solution processing: (i) water miscible solvents can be used to precipitate S8 to produce hydrophobic sulfur sols (Weimarn sols) [33,36], (ii) some solvents can show temperature dependent solubility and hence, upon cooling sulfur precipitates (this phenomenon is designated in this manuscript as dissolution–recrystallization) [37,38] or (iii) sulfur dissolution can be maintained in nucleophilic solvents in which sulfur precipitates upon acid addition (e.g. HCl or CH3COOH) [39,40]. Dissolution-recrytallization strategy can be an effective synthesis methodology to produce core–shell or hybrid structure formation from sulfur. However, this process has limitations. Firstly, elemental sulfur exhibits limited solubility in the vast majority of organic solvents [24]. There are limited solvents that can be used to dissolve sulfur like dimethyl sulfoxide [41], carbon disulfide (CS2) [42], toluene [4344] and ethylenediamine (EDA) [39,45,46] monoethanolamine [37], decanol and octanol [38], some ionic liquids [35] and n-octane [32]. CS2 seems to be the most suitable candidate for its high sulfur solubility (35.5 g sulfur per 100 g at 25 °C [35]). Along with its neurotoxicity, it has a low boiling point (46.3 °C), which prevents it from using in temperature dependent precipitation processes. Ionic liquids are relatively expensive and complicated solvents. Toluene, deconol, octanol or n-ocatane like solvents are hydrohobic solvents, and these solvents are not good dispersion mediums for particles like CNTs or graphene especially when these materials are functionalized. Nucleophilic solvents like EDA have also limitations. EDA is volatile. Though amine solvents have high solubility for sulfur, precipitation of sulfur requires acid addition. Depending upon the functionalization degree and type of carbons, low pH values (due to HCl addition) may induce agglomeration of particles. So sulfur composite production can be limited in solvents utilizing acid mediated precipitation method. Secondly, sulfur composite production through dissolution and recrystallization generally does not end up as desired in a single step. Namely, Tian et al. dissolved sulfur in toluene at 110 °C, then the dissolved sulfur was crystallized on functionalized MWCNTs [43]. Wang et al. [39] dissolved EDA which is dropwisely added to MWCNTs suspension (0.1 M HCl solution), by which they could be able to coat the surface of MWCNTs with sulfur. Fan et al. dispersed and dissolved CNTs and sulfur in acetone and then recrystallized sulfur on CNTs in acetone [47]. Sun also used EDA to deposite sulfur on acetylene black [40].

All of these methods depicts a process that covers the surface of carbon materials with sulfur which require a second step such as coating with conductive polymers to confine sulfur [39]. A better approach would be one step production of composite particles in which components homogenously mix with each other or encapsulation of sulfur is attained by additives.

In this study, it was demonstrated that sulfur has temperature dependent solubility in AcAc. Sulfur recrystallites upon cooling in AcAc. AcAc was found to be advantageous over other solvent in terms of composite particle production: (i) AcAc is not a volatile solvent as in the case of EDA (EDA releases toxic vapors to the environment), (ii) it does not require pH regulation to precipitate sulfur, (iii) it has a relatively high boiling point (140 °C), which enables high temperature operation, and (iv) AcAc is a good dispersion medium for nanoparticles partially due to its high dielectric constant (25) unlike toluene. Overall, AcAc provides an environment for homogenous dispersion of additives in sulfur particles or encapsulation of sulfur by carbonaceous additives during precipitation.

In this work, CQDs/polymer condensates (CQD-PC), commercial pristine CNTs and graphite were used as additives for sulfur composite particle production. As far as author knows for the first time in literature, this work highlights a strategy to produce sulfur composite/hybrid particles via sulfur-acetylacetone based solutions (heating and rapid cooling in AcAc colloid). It is speculated that this coating/ composite making strategy could be applied to other materials as long as homogenous dispersion and a certain level of interaction is maintained between sulfur and dispersed materials.

The reagents were analytical grade, purchased from Merck and used without further purification including ethylene glycol (EG) (≥99.5%), sulfuric acid (SA) (95.0–97.0 %) dichloromethane (DM) (≥99.8%), acetonitrile (ACN) (≥99.8%) and AcAc (≥99.0%) and graphite fine powder (Particle size (<50 µm)). Sulfur powder was purchased from ZAG Kimya (≥99.5%, Turkey). Multi wall carbon nanotubes were purchased from Thomas Swan & Co. Ltd. UK that have 30 nm average diameter and 30 µm length. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw: 40 kDa) and Sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (SDBS) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich.

Custom made Teflon lined reaction vessel (50 mL) was used. In a typical experiment, EG and SA were slowly mixed. The obtained solution put into autoclave and sealed. The reaction took place at 200 °C 3 h in a preheated oven. EG/SA mole ratio was 8. The filling fraction of the autoclaves were 22.3 V %. The concentration of SA in reaction mixture is 0.022 mol. The product was a viscous dark liquid. It was directly mixed with DM and distilled water. Most of the product contains hydrophobic substances and tend to disperse in DM. The product was rinsed with distilled water several times by decanting water. Subsequently, dichloromethane was removed from the system by using a rotary evaporator and powder samples were obtained. It is to be noted that elemental sulfur and/or volatile sulfides are generated as by-products during carbonization. These substances dissolve in DM and at high temperature/low pressure rotary evaporation (∼80 °C), they could be removed from the system along with dichloromethane. The obtained precipitate was dried under high vacuum at 80 °C. Since dialysis was not used, the obtained CQD powder is combination of CQDs, polymer condensates and low molecular weight hydrocarbons. It the manuscript, this CQD-polymer condensate combination is designated as CQD-PC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQD colloids for spectroscopic analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, 1 g L-1 CQD-PS powder was dispersed in ACN in ultrasonic bath. The obtained dispersions were centrifuged at 6000 rpm (revolutions per minute) for 5 min and the supernatants were collected. The collected supernatants were passed through a Teflon syringe filter (450 nm cut-off). It turned out that, supernatant of 1g L-1 CQD-PC was too concentrated to be analyzed with spectrofluorometer or UV–Vis spectrometer. Thereby, colloids were diluted 100 times.

For the preparation of carbon–sulfur composite, CQD-PC powder was used without further purification or filtration. 10 g L-1 CQD-PS were dispersed in 10 mL AcAc in glass vial by using an ultrasonic homogenizer (Bandelin HD 3100 equipped with MS 73 tip) for 30 min. 100 µL NaOH (0.25 M) was added prior to sonication. 5 mL of this suspension is taken and used. Sulfur powder (5 g L-1) was added to the dispersion. The vial was heated to 100 °C in an oil bath for 15 min during stirring on a hot plate. Then, the sulfur dissolved colloid immediately soaked in ice cold water and kept there for 15 min during stirring in another hot plate. The obtained liquid was mixed with water and centrifuged (There is no special need for mixing with water. It is possible to centrifuge the samples without mixing with water). Supernatants were removed and precipitates were collected and dried at 60 °C. Designations were done as follows: Sulfur particles that were produced by dissolving sulfur in AcAc is designated as S-Na. Na stands for NaOH. CQD-PC containing sulfur particles are designated as S-CQD or S-CQD-Na.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CNTs coating on sulfur particles
ce:section.ce:para: Same experimental steps were carried out as in the case of Sulfur/ CQD-PC composite for the production of CNT coated sulfur particles. The exceptions are the lack of NaOH and CQD-PC. Three types of CNT suspensions were produced. In all cases, CNTs concentration was 0.5 g L-1. In the first case CNTs are dispersed in AcAc without using dispersants. In other cases, 0.5 g L-1 PVP or 0.5 g L-1 SDBS were used as dispersants. The designations are as follows: S-CNT, S-CNT-PVP and S-CNT-SDBS. The schematic diagram of prominent steps of particle production is shown in Fig. 1 .

Same experimental steps were carried out as in the case of Sulfur/CQD-PC composite for the production of graphite-sulfur composites. Two experiments were carried out. Graphite and SDBS contents in AcAc are 0.5 g L-1 in one experiment and 2 g L-1 for the second experiment. Namely, graphite and SDBS contents were identical prior to precipitation. The analyzed sample is designated as S-Gr-SDBS.

Solubility of sulfur was measured qualitatively by dissolving different amounts of sulfur in AcAc and determining precipitation behavior by visual inspection. Sulfur solutions were designated as S-AcAc. Purity of purchased sulfur powder was checked by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) system (Hitachi SII6000 Exstar) and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) as shown in Fig. S1. As expected sulfur sublimes under nitrogen atmosphere below 350 °C with a 10 min s−1 heating rate. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis were performed by Zeiss Evo LS10. Micrographs that were taken with backscattered electron detector (four quadrant semiconductor detector) are designated as BSE image and the images that were acquired with secondary electron detector (Everhart–Thornley detector) are labeled as SE image. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDS) analyses were conducted by using EDAX-Element EDS system equipped with SEM. Samples were gold–palladium coated with a sputter coater (Quorum SC7620) prior to SEM analyses. XRD analyses were carried out by using PANalytical X’pert PRO diffractometer. Scans were taken in 2θ range of 5–65 with time per step of 0.0170°. Scan step time was selected as 59.69 s. Receiving slit and divergence slits were 1/2° and 1°, respectively. The soller slit is 10 mm in length and 100 mm mask was used. The X-ray beam was generated from a Cu target (1.54060 Å). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) studies were carried out with a Jeol JEM 2100 at the operating voltage of 200 kV. AFM studies were carried out using Shimadzu SPM-9600 under tapping mode using AGAR Scientific pyramidal silicon etched AFM probe. Prior to analyses, colloid samples were dried on cleaved mica substrates. Filtered colloids (450 nm) were used in AFM and TEM analyses. Photoluminescence (PL) behavior of colloids and solutions were analyzed by using QM-4/2003 QuantaMaster Steady-State Fluorescence System. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) was obtained by Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100 using Diamond/Zinc Selenide ATR (25 scans each with 4 cm−1 resolution). All the samples were analyzed in spectral ranges between 4000 cm−1 and 650 cm−1. However, relevant regions of the spectra are provided in figures. It is to be pointed out that, the device is in standard configuration, the spectral range does not allow for observation of S-S related bonds, which are found at low-energy far-infrared region [48]. UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy was performed in quartz cuvettes using PGeneral T80 + . Origin 2018 software was used for drawing of graphics. Dynamic light scattering analyses were conducted in Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS. A glass cuvette was used in all experiments. Pure AcAc, 1 g L-1 sulfur containing AcAc and 10 g L-1 sulfur containing AcAc liquids were analyzed. Solutions were not filtered prior to DLS analyses. Polystyrene latex sphere (Thermo scientific Nanosphere 300 nm size standard) suspension was prepared by dilution with deionized water. Raman spectroscopic analyses were performed with Perkin Elmer 400F spectrometer (laser wavelength 785 nm). Each result was obtained by averaging 10 scans that have duration of 5 s. Spectra are shown between 120 and 750-cm−1 region because the relevant bands were present in that spectral range. Solutions were obtained by heating to 100 °C prior to analyses and glass vials containing solutions were placed inside Raman spectrometer immediately. During the Raman analysis, solutions cool and especially 25 g L-1 sulfur dissolved solution formed certain amount of precipitation. Precipitation did not create unreliability to the Raman measurements since the analyses took place for short time periods, and Raman focus was not obtained at the bottom of the vials where precipitate accumulation might take place.

Precipitation of supersaturated sulfur solutions dictated designation of initially 10 g L-1 S containing suspensions as “sulfur saturated solution” in process of explaining UV–Vis, ATR-FTIR and DLS analyses.

The mechanism of particle production is illustrated in Fig. 1. Sulfur dissolution and recrystallization can be triggered by consecutive heating and cooling. This strategy was found to not only allow for sulfur crystal formation, but also it is possible to make composites when solution contains suitable homogenously dispersed particles. Hence, during recrystallization of sulfur, it interacts with surrounding particles and co-precipitate to form sulfur composite particles or core–shell sulfur particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of dissolution behavior of sulfur in AcAc
ce:section.ce:para: Knowing behavior of sulfur in polar and nonvolatile solvents is important because it opens up new possibilities to prepare new hybrid materials. AcAc can dissolve 1–2 g L-1 sulfur at room temperature. Sulfur has temperature dependent solubility in AcAc. It was found that AcAc can dissolve more than 25 g L-1 S8 at 100 °C, and if solution is slowly cooled to room temperature, it can stay supersaturated for a few minutes (if it is left undisturbed) as given in Fig. 2 a. At first glance the underlying mechanism behind high sulfur solubility was thought to be due to carbonyl groups (possible nuclephilicity of AcAc), but color of AcAc upon dissolution of sulfur is different than occuring in other nucleophilic solvents. It is known that particularly in nucleophilic solvent solutions, polysulfides should form, which induces concentration dependent coloration. The blue or red colors could be associated with [S3]•‾ radical anions or [S6]2‾ anions, respectively [35]. As shown in Fig. S2 and Fig. 2a, sulfur added AcAc has yellow/orange color. The yellow coloration may implies that hydrophobic interactions take place between sulfur and AcAc. There is an exception. When NaOH is introduced, the solution color changes from yellow to red at high temperature (80 °C-100 °C), indicating the evolution of sodium polysulfides (NaxS) in solution. To elucidate the solubility behavior of sulfur several characterization techniques were employed. UV–Vis spectra of S-AcAcs are shown in Fig. 2b. Due to the presence of lone pairs of electrons on carbonyl groups in AcAc, UV cut-off wavelength of this solvent is at about 400 nm. Thereby, it could not be possible to see band gap transition of zero valent sulfur (334 nm) [49], octasulfur: S8 (270 nm) [37], or other seemingly possible bands (316 nm, 400 nm) related to sulfur radical ions and polysulfides [39]. S-AcAcs possess two bands at 468 cm−1 and ∼ 498 cm−1, that are accounting for the yellow color of the solutions. The band at 498 nm might be assigned to S4 [50]. The results are different than that of obtained with sulfur-amine chemistry. Namely, polysulfide peak at 616 nm is absent in S-AcAcs [39]. So according to UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, the solubility of sulfur may not be result of nucleophilic interactions.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of sulfur containing solutions do not possess absorption tail extending to the infrared region, which may be the result of being in a solubilized state. Because the presence of absorption tails extending beyond UV–Vis region may be related to light scattering from particles. There is an exception. Namely, sulfur quantum dots do not possess adsorption tail [49 51], but sulfur quantum dots exhibit PL emission. Then the question arises: Do S-AcAc solutions have PL? S-AcAc solutions have insignificant PL emission behavior as shown in Fig. 2c. Because firstly, even pure AcAc has a more intense PL than S-AcAc solutions. Secondly, solutions have insignificant PL intensities compared to a PL reference such as CQD-PC used in this study (inset in Fig. 2c). Solution behavior is scrutinized with DLS (Fig. 2d). DLS was designed to characterize suspensions. It relies on scattering of light. The instrument produces correlation function by interpreting backscattered photons from particles. The y-intercept in the correlation function can give a clue about the state of dispersion. A low y-intercept value can be an indication of (i) multiple scattering from particles, (ii) sample absorbance or (iii) fluorescence [52]. If a solution is being analyzed by DLS, y-intercept should be very low. The results were benchmarked against polystyrene latex aqueous suspension that gives a y-intercept of more than 0.8. On the other hand, sulfur saturated AcAc and AcAc has very low y-intercept value, which is lower than 0.2. The other possibilities for low y-intercept are tenuous. Namely, solutions show negligible fluorescence, and multiple scattering may not be the reason. Because even 25 g L-1 sulfur dissolved AcAc is clear and transulent. DLS and UV–Vis analyses confirms that sulfur can dissolve in AcAc.

The other important aspect of S-AcAc is about chemical state of sulfur. Normally, sulfur exists as S8 rings at its standard state [53,54]. It is known that S-S bond breakage occurs at temperatures above 150 °C [7]. However, amine nucleophilic activators are known to induce homolysis of S bonds at low temperatures [54,55]. So it is possible to form sulfur chain species at room temperature. It was proposed that nucleophilic attack of nitrogen in amines accounts for formation of alkylammonium polysulfides [54,56,57]. Nucleophilicity was found to be key process to provide solubility of sulfur in amine solvents [35]. Overall, sulfur can be in the form of homocyclic rings or in the form of charged linear molecules. To have a better understanding about the chemical state of sulfur and its dissolution ability in AcAc, ATR-FTIR was used as shown in Fig. 2e. To avoid misleading assessment, infrared spectra of only 1 g L-1 sulfur loaded AcAc and pure AcAc are compared. Because it was found that IR spectra of precipitating sulfur solutions may lead to misinterpretation (Fig. S3). Further discussion is provided in supplementary data. Infrared specta of 1 g L-1 sulfur loaded AcAc, and pure AcAc are identical. This means that there is no cleavage of homocyclic rings, and there is no interaction of sulfur species with carbonyl groups at least at low sulfur contents at room temperature.

Raman analyses results of S-AcAc solutions, sulfur powder and AcAc are shown in Fig. 2f. S-AcAc solutions possess Raman peaks at 150 cm−1, 217 cm−1 and 476 cm−1, which are absent in sulfur-free AcAc (These peaks are present, but barely discernable in 1 g L-1 sulfur containing solution). The peaks are related to homocyclic ring fundamental vibrations of sulfur except peaks of AcAc [31,58]. Thereby, the state of sulfur can be considered similar to found in hydrophobic solvents like CS2 [58]. Raman intensity of the bands become stronger as sulfur content increases, and no additional bands appear in the spectra with variation in sulfur content. Based on Raman analysis results, it can be concluded that sulfur exists in the form of homocyclic rings (e.g. S8, S4) in AcAc at around room temperature and probably up to floor temperature of sulfur where temperature would be sufficient enough to form sulfur radical chain species. It is to be noted that AcAc may allow formation of chain species (sulfur anions) in its structure by the assistance of additives at relatively low temperatures. Namely, as indicated above, NaOH addition induced color change from yellow to red in AcAc. This red coloration of the solution is believed to be the result of ring opening reaction to form NaSx salts (The dissolution of NaOH and color change of S-AcAc solution was performed at 100 °C). The red color of the solution slowly fades away after exposure to air. Because NaSx species are sensitive to air.

Organic precursors such as polyols can be used to prepare various heteroatom doped carbon materials including CQDs, carbon foams or functionalized carbon nano or micro particles. H2SO4 can be used as a dehydrating/carbonization agent [6,59,60]. Each synthesis parameter affects the final structure. It was shown to be possible to prepare carbon sheets [61] or CQDs by varying precursor ratios using glycerol as carbon source [62]. As in the case of glycerol, diethylene glycol and small amount of H2SO4 could be used to synthesize CQDs [63]. Similarly, EG can be a suitable starting material for carbon material synthesis. It may not only serve as a reaction medium for the synthesis [64], but also acts as a carbon source for various types of carbon materials production. Interestingly, examples related to EG usage for synthesis of CQDs are limited [65,66]. In the previous study, the author studied on production of sulfonated bulk carbon material from EG by using excess amount of H2SO4 with respect to EG [67]. It was found that EG/SA weight ratio more than unity (under solvothermal conditions) enables formation of CQD (or polymer condensate) dominant solvothermal products rather than formation of functionalized bulk carbons. Because the decrease in driving force for chemical reactions in the reaction mixture allows CQDs or polymer condensates like sensitive materials to be generated without allowing further carbonization. This is the main reason for choosing EG/SA weight ratio of 8. Morphological and elemental analyses on CQD-PC powder sample were conducted by using SEM equipped with EDS detector (Fig. 3 a-e). Powder is composed of irregular shape particles (Fig. 3a). At relatively high magnifications, it can be seen that some regions contain small spherical particles with sizes in the range of 2 µm (Fig. 3b). It is not possible to discern contents of the powder at this state. Therefore, filtration was employed to differentiate the components. When CQD-PC suspension is filtered with Teflon membrane (220 nm cut-off), polymer condensates are revealed which have sizes in the range of 200–300 nm (Fig. 3c-d). Fig. 3e shows EDS spectrum in logarithmic scale. The sample contains 10.1 wt% oxygen and 4.5 wt% sulfur in its structure. It seems that produced carbon is heteroatom doped. I was also observed that sample is an electrical insulator. Because it requires relatively thick conductive coating prior to SEM analysis. This implies that the material is composed of functionalized entangled polymer-like aliphatic hydrocarbons.

To signify the functional groups and chemical structure, ATR-FTIR (Fig. 3f1 and f2) analyses were used. The bands at 3000–2800 cm−1 can be assigned to aliphatic carbon, (i.e., C–H stretching vibrations (Fig. 3d1) [68]. It is known that ester carbonyl absorption frequency is higher than normal ketones (1725 cm−1) [69]. Hence, the shoulder at 1734 cm−1 is related to carbonyl groups in esters, which is an expected functional group. Because after reaction, material has a characteristic fruity odor. The intense band around 1699 cm−1 is related to carbonyls. The bands at 1634 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 cannot be related to H–O–H bending vibration. Because the sample is hydrophobic (it tends to disperse in those organic solvents having low dielectric constant). Those bands may be related to C = C stretch in alkenes. The shoulder at 1575 cm−1 may be ascribed to aromatic C = C stretch [68]. The absorption bands at 1454 cm−1 and 1374 cm−1 may be the result of C–H bending vibrations. Note the concurrent presence of bands around 2900 cm−1 and the bands at 1454 cm−1 and 1374 cm−1, they are all indicative of aliphatic regions in the sample. The bands around 1220 cm−1 can be ascribed to sulfonic acid groups [70] . The sharp peak at 1032 cm−1 can be associated with O = S = O stretching vibrations [71] or only S = O stretching. The peak at 965 cm−1 can be attributed to C = C bending or C–H bending in alkenes. In addition, presence of aliphatic ethers or esters may have been manifested with the peaks at 1244 cm−1 (C–O–C asymmetrical stretch or C–O stretch) and 1086 cm−1 (C–O stretch). Overall, the sample both contains polar (C = O) and non-polar (C–H) functional groups, which may cause the material be amphiphilic.

XRD pattern is shown in Fig. 3g. The hump located at around 20.2° belongs (002) diffraction peak of graphite. It is an indication of significant topological disorder in sample [72], which means that along the longitudinal dimension, there is scarcity in stacking of carbon sheets. The other peak is at around 12°. This peak is an indication of structural disorder in sample. These peak positions reveals that the interlayer spacing is greater than that of graphite (0.3354 nm [73]). These layers are not stacked in a parallel fashion and therefore there may be small pores or voids between them [73].There is also a very weak (100) Bragg peak in XRD pattern. This indicates that sp2 hybridized regions are very small in lateral directions (along the graphene planes) as well. It is to be noted that the sample or its solutions could not be analyzed with Raman spectroscopy even at different laser wavelengths due to strong PL (Photoluminescence) they possess.

Optical images demonstrates PL behavior of colloid under 360 nm UV-light irradiation (Fig. 4 a). TEM image revealed that colloid is composed of spherical particles that have sizes ranging from hundreds of nanometers to a tens of nanometers (Fig. 4a). It is known that synthesis products may contain many emissive and non-emissive byproducts [74], which cannot be simply removed by filtration unless dialysis is used. This is the reason that large particles are also present in TEM images. Dialysis could not be used for synthesized CQD-PC. The reason behind is explained in discussion section. These large particles are speculated to be polymer condensates [75], which may also exhibit PL. So the structure of EG-SA solvothermal products is believed to be combination of small and big particles. Diffraction pattern in inset accords with XRD pattern that the particles are amorphous. Fig. 4c shows the AFM image. As in the case of TEM image, the structure is composed of spherical particles, agglomerates of CQDs and/or polymer condensates. It is known that CQDs should have diameters around few nanometers [8]. AFM height profiles confirms that CQDs are present and have diameters below 10 nm as shown in Fig. 4c (C-D profile height). It should be noted that when interpreting AFM image for particle size measurement, height information (z direction) should be considered. Because radius of curvature of AFM tip complicates size measurement along x-y direction. As in the case of Raman analysis, strong fluorescence of CQD-PS does not allow measurement of particle size by DLS. Thereby, no DLS data is presented. In the previous study, author could be able to use DLS on solvothermal carbon colloid. Because particle size of that prepared colloid corresponds to agglomerates of CQDs and carbon nanoparticles [67]. To explore the optical properties and to confirm that CQDs are present in the product, UV–Vis absorption and PL emission spectra of diluted colloid were obtained at room temperature (Fig. 4e, f and g). UV–Vis absorption spectra is subtle having only one hump at 265 nm. Therefore, in the studied case, there is no distinct peaks that could be used to relate absorption peaks with excitation-emission behavior as observed in other studies [76]. Emission spectra of colloid in ACN are shown with respect to excitation wavelength in Fig. 4f. The maximum emission peak appeared at about 426 nm at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. There are multiple emission bands between 300 nm and 440 nm. The prominent emission peaks are at about 400 nm, 426 nm, and 452 nm (shoulder peak). It seems that depending on the excitation wavelength different emission centers play a dominating role. The colloid exhibits excitation wavelength dependent emission behavior. However, between excitations of 340 nm and 400 nm, the PL maximum becomes irrespective of excitation and appears at the same wavelength (426 nm). Sun et al, investigated the properties of CQDs and concluded that excitation dependence is the result of domination of different transitions under different excitations [76]. The findings in this study agrees well with that report. However, it should be noted that, though excitation dependent emission is common for many carbon dots, Papaioannou et al. measured photoluminescence spectra of freeze-dried hydrothermal carbon dots and revealed that excitation dependence may be due to particle-solvent interactions [72].

Surface properties of CQD-PC were also investigated because it may shed light on their interaction with surrounding environment (i.e., colloidal behavior). There are two possibilities: (i) functional groups that effect PL emission may be present on the surface or (ii) passivated layers can be present which may prevent surface states from being affected from the impurities in the surrounding medium. It is known that protonation or deprotonating of functional groups can lead to shift in PL [77]. To this extent, pH response of CQD-PS were analyzed and given in Fig. 4g. It is understood that fluorescence intensities are effected from pH. If pH is lower or higher than pH 7, emission intensity decreases. It is known that protonation of carboxyl groups leads to quenching of PL intensity [66]. This implies that, surface states take part in the fluorescence behavior of CQD-PS and they are not surrounded by passivated surface layers. It is to be noted that exact mechanism for the origin of fluorescence is not clear, and the process is a complex phenomenon. In this context, many emission mechanism were proposed in literature such as cross-linked enhanced emission [78] and quantum size effect [77,79]. Purification of CQDs is crucial to investigate emission mechanism [79]. However, tentative deductions about PL emission mechanism could be made by using available information for this study. ATR-FTIR analysis revealed that powder sample is significantly rich in aliphatic regions. So it is speculated that some of the -C–H bonds in linear chains of polymerized EG are replaced by sulfur atoms, which enable cross-linking just like in the case of vulcanization of rubber. These bridges provide rigidity to the system limiting vibration and rotation of the aliphatic domains. This eventually may cause PL emission due to cross-linked enhanced emission mechanism.

Optical images of precipitation behavior of sulfur by temperature regulation is shown in Fig. S2. S-Na precipitate is shown in Fig. 5 a. S-Na is composed of orthorhombic sulfur crystals and rod-shaped crystals with sizes around 2 µm to 20 µm. It is to be noted that, AcAc is not only a suitable solvent to dissolve sulfur, but also a good dispersion media for carbon particles. Because AcAc has a relatively high dielectric constant (25), which is one of the factors that enables homogeneous dispersion of particularly polar functional groups containing particles in AcAc. In addition, AcAc is immiscible with water due to its relatively large carbon backbone, and hence it may provide hydrophobic interactions with particles, which may allow homogeneous dispersion of hydrophobic materials. It was found that CQD-PC/AcAc colloid is an excellent environment to produce carbon–sulfur composite particles when homogenous dispersion is provided. The importance of homogenous dispersion of CQD-PC is understood by comparison of precipitates of H2SO4 added, NaOH added or NaOH-free colloids. Fig. 5b shows BSD image of dissolved and crystallized composite precipitate that is designated as S-CQD. Presence of CQD-PC leads to formation of well-crystallized orthorhombic sulfur crystals with sharp edges. Particles size range is about 10 µm. There is a minimal level of interaction between CQD-PC and sulfur particles during crystallization/precipitation in this case. Because there is segregation in the composite. Micrograph of a carbon agglomerate and corresponding EDS spectrum are examples to this segregation behavior (Fig. S4a-b). As shown in Fig. 3d2 and Fig. 4g, CQD-PC contains polar functional groups that are exposed to the surrounding medium. So protonated functional groups lead to agglomeration of CQD-PC just like in the case of functionalized CNTs [80]. Thereby, NaOH was used as a charger salt. Fig. 5c, d, e and f show electron micrographs of S-CQDs-Na. It is evident that addition of NaOH has a marked influence on precipitated sulfur crystals. There is a narrow particle size distribution which is at around 4 µm (histogram of particle size distribution of the image is shown in inset in Fig. 5c). All particles have quasi-spherical shape and some particles are hollow. High magnification secondary electron (SE) images reveal that particles have pomegranate-like morphology (Fig. 5f). Spherical micron sized particles are composed of smaller nanometer sized spherical particles. These smaller particles have diameters in the range of 200 nm (Histogram in Fig. 5f). These small particles are polymer condensates, which are blended with sulfur during recrystallization. Compared to S-Na, particle size is considerably decreased in the case of S-CQD-Na. Dispersed CQD-PC seems to provide nucleation sites for sulfur crystals, then particles grow until depletion of constituents. The key aspect of CQD-PCs is believed to be their amphiphilic properties. They contain –COOH groups which allow them to be dispersed homogenously when they are deprotonated. At the same time they interact with sulfur, because of their hydrophobic properties. The same experiment conducted with addition of H2SO4 or excess amount of NaOH addition as given in Fig. S5a and b, respectively. The resulting materials do not exhibit homogeneity due to segregation of CQD-PC in AcAc, which eventually leads to minimal level of interaction between CQD-PC and sulfur nucleates. This emphasizes the importance of homogenous dispersion of materials in liquid for successful composite making during precipitation.

To confirm that carbon is incorporated in sulfur particles, EDS and ATR-FTIR analyses were conducted. Fig. 6 a shows SE image of S-CQD-Na. Corresponding X-ray map is shown in Fig. 6b and in Fig. S6a and b. The particles are composed of mostly sulfur. Relatively high magnification image and corresponding elemental maps are provided in Fig. 6c-d and Fig. S6c and d, which confirm that a representative particle contains carbon. EDS spectra performed on S-CQDs and S-CQDs-Na are shown in Fig. 6e and corresponding elemental distributions are given in inset. Carbon constitutes roughly 30 wt% of particles in S-CQD-Na. On the other hand, S-CQD only contains ∼ 7 wt% carbon. ATR-FTIR spectra of sulfur, CQDs and S-CQD-Na are shown in Fig. 6f. Spectra of sulfur is subtle. On the other hand spectrum of S-CQD-Na contains peaks that can be associated with infrared peaks of CQD-PC.

Versatility of dissolution and precipitation strategy to produce sulfur composite particles is verified by using CNTs as well. Entangling nature of CNTs may enable rigidity to sulfur particles. CNTs are flexible, which enable them to withstand volume changes during charge–discharge of active materials in electrochemical applications. Besides, pristine CNTs are conductive. Therefore, it was found to be useful to use CNTs as composite component. It turned out CNTs tend to encapsulate sulfur particles unlike in the case of CQD-PC. Fig. 7 a shows micrograph of CNTs precipitate used in the study. CNTs precipitate was prepared by applying the same preparation conditions that are implemented to produce sulfur particles. So it can be put forward that there is no tendency of sulfur-free CNTs to entangle and form particles when heated and rapidly cooled in AcAc. Fig. 7b-d and Fig. S7a-f shows a representative S-CNT particles at different magnifications, which is prepared from dispersant-free suspension of CNTs. EDS spectrum of a selected area of one particle is included in Fig. 7d, which confirms that particle is composed of carbon and sulfur. The particle shapes are irregular and they can reach tens of micrometers in length. It is to be noted that the whole structure is not composed only of CNT encapsulated sulfur particles as Fig. S7e shows that aggregates of sulfur particles are present. There are two reasons for the observed phenomenon: selecting inappropriate CNT/Sulfur ratio and relatively low dispersion of CNTs in AcAc. For this reason, PVP and SDBS were used as dispersants since they are known to stabilize CNTs [81,82]. Fig. 7e-f and Fig. S8a-i shows S-PVP particles at different magnifications. Unlike in the case of S-CNT, CNT coated rhombic shaped crystals can be clearly seen in S-CNT-PVP. In addition, homogeneity of the structure is increased due to better dispersion of CNTs in AcAc though some uncoated sulfur particles are observed as given in Fig. S8h and i. BSE, SE images and EDS spectrum and mapping results (Fig. S9a-d) confirm presence of sulfur in CNTs encapsulated particles. SDBS also works well as shown in SEM images in Fig. S10a-d, e1 and f1. In the case of S-CNT-SDBS micron sized rod-like CNT encapsulated particles are present. Both S-CNT-PVP (Fig. S9b-d) and S-CNT SDBS (Fig. S10e1 and f1) show variation of sulfur content at different regions. This means that CNT coating thickness differ in different particles.

The effect of graphite on precipitation behavior of sulfur was also investigated. SEM images are shown in Fig. S11. Though there is some degree of segregation due to inadequate dispersion of graphite in AcAc, SEM images proves that there is also tendency of graphite flakes to encapsulate sulfur particles as in the case of CNTs. Additional information is provided in supplementary data. Ghosh et al. prepared graphene oxide and sulfur composite by polyethyleneimine addition by using Weimarn sol technique and used the material in electrochemical applications [36]. In that study the BSE images of the precipitated sol is very similar to the observed with S-Gr-SDBS precipitate. It is to be noted that S-CQD and S-CNT precipitates have even better dispersion character than S-Gr-SDBS. Overall, these results imply that dissolution and recrystallization in high dielectric constant solvents may be a feasible option for composite particle production.

In this article CQD-PC were successfully incorporated in sulfur particles. However, to maintain clarity, information regarding purity of CQD-PC powder should be given, since all the additives affect final structure of S-CQDs. Namely, CQD powders were not subjected to dialysis. It is known that as prepared CQDs powders or solutions may contain molecular by-products [74] such as aliphatic hydrocarbons. Dialysis is most well-known method to purify CQDs. Generally dialysis is conducted in aqueous solvents. In the studied case, the products are not soluble in water. They tend to dissolve in organic solvents. DM, isopropyl alcohol, acetone or ethanol were tried as dialysis solvents. Unfortunately, dialysis bags (regenerated cellulose) became hardened and did not allow selective penetration of low molecular weight by-products. Thereby, this study is conducted by using as-synthesized solvothermal products. As a result the product is combination of CQDs and polymer condensates and low molecular weight hydrocarbons. This was the reason of keeping S/CQDs mass ratio at 0.5 in precipitation application (i.e., 10 g L-1 CQD-PC and 5 g L-1 sulfur in AcAc prior to recrystallization). Considerable amount of carbonaceous material remained in supernatant after centrifugation as shown in Fig. S12a. The reason for this peculiarity is not clearly understood, it may be the result of using a composite powder (CQDs + polymer condensates) as carbon source. It is understood by trial and error approaches that CQD-PC concentration should be higher than that of sulfur in the colloid to better control particle size, particle shape and homogeneity of the precipitated structure. ATR-FTIR analysis were performed on supernatant and precipitate to see the reason behind this phenomenon (Fig. S12b). IR spectrum of CQD-PC is more similar to IR spectrum of supernatant than that of precipitate. One reason for the difference in IR spectra is, presence of sulfur decreases the IR signal intensity. The second reason is speculated to be result of hydrophobic interactions of sulfur with less polar molecules in CQD-PC solution during precipitation. Because when signal intensity ratios of hydrophobic (-C-H: ∼2900 cm−1) and hydrophilic regions (–COOH) are compared in each spectra, it transpires that peak intensity of hydrophobic regions in spectra of precipitate is more pronounced than hydrophilic regions unlike that of the supernatant. So it seems that sulfur nucleates prefer hydrophobic materials during crystallization. Composite precipitate is dispersed/dissolved in AcAc and analyzed with AFM (Fig. S12c-d). It is understood that precipitate is composed of irregular shape particles and spherical particles, which can be deemed as CQDs and polymer condensates.

The precipitate mass after centrifugation was 0.011 g for 5 mL liquid for S-CQD-Na. Considering that ∼ 65 wt% of the precipitate is composed of sulfur (EDS analysis), 0.022 g sulfur should present in the precipitate without any material loss. This transpires that 50 wt% of sulfur remains in the precipitate of S-CQD-Na. So for 1 L of liquid, 2.2 g composite can be produced.

Another aspect to be discussed is about NaOH addition to CQD-PC suspension. It is evident that NaOH addition has a profound effect on composite structure. NaOH addition increased CQD-PC concentration in composite particles (Fig. 6e). There should be two likely stabilization mechanism of CQD-PC The first mechanism is charger salt effect. For pH values greater than 7, the bulk sulfur powder is likely to be dissolved as shown in the reaction (1) [51,83]:
(1)
3 S + 6 N a O H = 2 N
a 2
S + N
a 2
S
O 3
+ 2
H 2

Presence of sodium polysulfides (NaxS) is evident by the color change in sulfur solutions (Fig. S2). Thereby, presence of NaxS (or Sx 2-) and Na2SO3 may have influence on dispersion of CQD-PC (hindered agglomeration). In this respect, these chemicals act as charger salts by improving electrical double layer around CQD-PC. Second and more likely possibility is deprotonation of functional groups in CQD-PC. It should be noted that NaOH was included to CQD-PC suspension prior to sulfur addition. So the functional groups may have deprotonated (e.g., –COO–). This may have provided the required double layer around particles to prevent agglomeration. Finally, it was found that cooling regime, heat transfer, degree of dispersion of CQD-PC, pH, presence of other chemicals influences sulfur composite particle size, shape, and homogeneity. For example, it was found that excess or insufficient NaOH addition disrupts the integrity of composite particles leading to phase separation (Fig. S5b).

Precipitation behavior of S-CNTs is different compared to CQD-PC (Fig. S13a-d). S-CNTs tend to precipitate at the water-AcAc interphase. However, in the case of PVP containing suspensions, the precipitate smears along walls of tube from top to bottom. The reason for formation of precipitate at the interphase is partially due to hydrophobic nature of CNTs, the reason for precipitation without being influenced by the water/AcAc interphase is due to hydrophilic nature of PVP or PVP modified CNTs.

As given in Fig. S13e, in S-Gr-SDBS, precipitation pattern reveal the segregation behavior of the precipitate (some regions are black and some regions are grey). This observation may be used to confirm homogenous dispersion of composites because it is already known through SEM analyses that there is segregation in S-Gr-SDBS.

As already pointed out, CQD-PC and CNTs show different distribution behavior. This poses the question, why CQD-PCs tend to disperse in sulfur particles, but CNTs or graphite tend to wrap the surface of sulfur particles? In the preliminary studies with CQD-PC, it was observed that CQD-PCs tend to wrap the surface of sulfur particles as well (Fig. S14a-b). Namely, polymer condensates favored clustering on sulfur particles in core–shell particles (Fig. S14c) rather than mixing with sulfur. So the governing factor for homogeneous dispersion or accumulation of sulfur-AcAc interphase is not related to presence of polymer condensates. This finding implies that during recrystallization, polymer condensates‘ interaction with sulfur can be altered by other additives present in colloid. The difference between used CQD-PCs in preliminary experiments and this current study is the CQD-PCs were not extensively washed with water in preliminary experiments. So they contain more water dispersible relatively polar organic compounds. So as a result less water washed CQD-PCs do not favor interaction with sulfur as high as excessively water washed ones. Overall, interaction degree between sulfur and additives or carbons is believed to govern the formation of different carbon distribution patterns. It is speculated that if chemical state of sulfur particles and carbon material are close, they tend to form homogenous composite particles. Because sulfur carry out specific interaction with additives/carbons during nucleation and crystal growth constantly and hence, both sulfur and carbons condense concurrently. However, if there is a difference in interaction tendency, the result would be wrapping of sulfur with carbon materials during recrystallization (This does not mean polar materials like TiO2 would work the same way) to lower the surface energy of sulfur crystallites. The other reason for tendency of carbon materials to encapsulate sulfur may be due to aspect ratio of carbons. CNTs entangle and may not work as nucleation sites as in the case of CQD-PC. In this context, it is speculated that layered materials such as graphene or graphite may also encapsulate sulfur particles. So the experiments were conducted using graphite as well. As expected graphite also has a tendency to cluster at the sulfur-AcAc interface.

It is to be noted that for successful application, sulfur/carbon material ratio should be appropriate. Excess amount of sulfur may lead to segregation. One drawback of this technique is waste production. Fortunately, AcAc can be reused by distillation.

The studies are under development and requires further work. However, this study proves that dissolution and recrystallization in AcAc enable production of core–shell sulfur hybrid particles. A workflow is generated to produce sulfur composite particles as shown in Fig. S15. Finally, it is to be noted that these technique may not be universal, it may not work on many materials. It is speculated that it may be harder to provide interactions between polar materials such as TiO2 or Al2O3 and sulfur. Fortunately, dispersants may provide necessary interaction with sulfur crystallites.

In summary, this study proves that it is possible to wrap the surface of sulfur particles with carbon or polymeric particles during recrystallization in a colloid. CNT or graphite encapsulated sulfur particles or quasi-spherical sulfur-CQD-polymer condensate composite particles are produced using recrystallization of sulfur in AcAc. It was found that sulfur has a temperature dependent solubility, and it exists as rings species (i.e. S4 etc.) in AcAc as confirmed via Raman spectroscopy. Dissolution of sulfur at high temperature, and rapid cooling in carbon colloids of AcAc enabled production of composite carbon–sulfur particles. Hydrothermally produced hydrophobic CQD-PC particles enable production of carbon–sulfur particles that have quasi-spherical shapes with particle sizes in the range of 4 µm. On the other hand when particularly CNTs are used, carbon materials wrap the surface of sulfur particles during recrystallization. This study demonstrates a facile method to produce encapsulated micron sized sulfur particles. It was also observed that there is a certain degree of segregation in structures during production of core–shell particles, which emphasizes the importance of some factors effecting efficiency of particle formation. Namely, molecular level interactions during recrystallization of sulfur, dispersion degree of carbon material in AcAc, aspect ratio, size and quantity are needed to be considered for successful operation. It is believed that this study has significance for other works that require production of sulfur composite particles or encapsulated sulfur particles. In addition, it is speculated that AcAc-sulfur chemistry can provide a platform for producing organosulfur compounds as well.
Ali Can Zaman: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.
